medications.drug_name: aspirin or any other medication given at home before assessment
medications.indication_rationale: Antiplatelet or other medication should not be given until haemorrhage has been excluded by CT imaging in hospital, since aspirin would be harmful if this turns out to be a haemorrhagic stroke; this decision belongs to the stroke team, not telehealth.
contraindications_in_this_case: Haemorrhagic stroke has not yet been excluded by imaging

medications.drug_name: any oral fluids, food, or medications by mouth
medications.indication_rationale: Strict nil-by-mouth is essential given the high risk of aspiration from probable swallowing difficulty (dysphagia) associated with the stroke.
contraindications_in_this_case: High aspiration risk from likely dysphagia

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep the patient seated safely, strictly nil by mouth, and note (or write down) the exact time symptoms started to tell the paramedics.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Establishing and communicating the precise last-known-well time is critical for treatment eligibility; nil-by-mouth reduces aspiration risk.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: If the patient's level of consciousness declines, position him in the recovery position on his non-paralysed (left) side to protect the airway.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Protects the airway from aspiration if consciousness deteriorates before paramedics arrive.

patient_education_points.point: The sudden facial drooping, arm and leg weakness, and inability to speak clearly together strongly suggest he is having a major stroke — this needs an emergency brain scan and possible clot-dissolving treatment as fast as possible.
Please call 000 right now and specifically mention a suspected stroke with the exact time it started (about 45 minutes ago) — this will activate a stroke code so the hospital can prepare.
Please don't give him anything to eat or drink, not even water, as his swallowing may be affected and this risks it going into his lungs.
If he becomes less responsive, help him into the recovery position on his left (non-paralysed) side to protect his airway, and update the 000 operator.
Try to stay calm and let the paramedics do the talking with him — he understands more than he can express right now.

follow_up_plan.interval: Superseded by emergency escalation — follow-up arranged by the emergency and stroke team after treatment
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Post-discharge: formal swallowing assessment, rehabilitation planning (physiotherapy, speech therapy), anticoagulation restart discussion with cardiology, secondary stroke prevention
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Declining consciousness or worsening deficit while awaiting the ambulance — position in recovery position on the left side and update 000.

must_include_items: Call 000 immediately and explicitly request a stroke-code/Priority 1 ambulance to a stroke-capable hospital
Establish and clearly communicate the precise last-known-well time
Strict nil by mouth given aspiration risk
Safety-netting with recovery-position guidance if consciousness declines
acceptable_alternatives: None — given the strict time-critical thrombolysis/thrombectomy windows, immediate ambulance dispatch specifically requesting stroke-code activation is the only acceptable first action
errors_of_omission: Failure to establish or communicate the last-known-well time
errors_of_commission: Recommending self-transport by car instead of ambulance
Giving or recommending oral fluids/food/medication before assessment
Attempting to differentiate the exact stroke subtype via telehealth before escalating (delaying the call)
Recommending an outpatient neurology referral instead of emergency stroke-code activation
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: CRITICAL CASE: Any response that does not include (1) immediate 000 call with explicit stroke-code activation AND (2) establishing/communicating the last-known-well time is an automatic FAIL. This is a T5 case with a strict, narrowing treatment window.
